title: Around-the-Tree Bench
summary: An octagonal bench built as eight identical leg-and-deck sections, assembled around a tree trunk — with a hexagon variant noted for a smaller or more angular tree.
description:
This bench is built as eight identical sections rather than one continuous ring, which is what makes it possible to build around an existing tree instead of a fixed circular frame — build all eight leg sets first, then all eight deck sections, and only bring the whole thing together around the trunk at final assembly. **Prestain every board before assembly** — the plan is explicit that finishing this project after it's built is genuinely difficult, since the octagon's inside faces become hard to reach once the sections are joined.

Each leg set is a 4x4 with a 2x4 top cap and beveled 2x4 side pieces mitered at 22.5° so eight of them ring a full octagon; add blocking inside each leg set to tie the top cap and sides together solidly. Lay out the full bench on a flat, level surface first to dry-fit every deck board before final assembly — building blind, straight around the actual tree, makes mistakes much harder to catch and correct.

**A hexagon (6-sided) version is a genuine variant, not an afterthought**: build six leg sets instead of eight, cut the side pieces at 30° instead of 22.5°, and cut the deck boards' end angles at 30° as well — everything else about the build carries over unchanged.

cutList.note: One end cut at 22.5° bevel off square, long point measurement.
Long point measurement, both ends cut at 22.5° off square.
Short point measurement, both ends cut at 22.5° off square.
Long point measurement, both ends cut at 22.5° off square.
Long point measurement, both ends beveled at 22.5° off square.

steps.title: Cut every part first
steps.body: This bench is eight identical sections, so cut in batches with a stop block rather than measuring each piece — identical parts are what make the octagon close. From the 4x4s cut sixteen legs at 16-1/2", eight per 12 ft board. From the 2x4s cut eight leg-set tops at 19", sixteen leg-set sides at 18" with one end beveled 22.5deg off square to the long point, and eight 3-1/2" blocks; note that these total 468" and will need a fourth 10 ft 2x4 beyond the three listed. From the 2x6s cut eight outer deck boards at 26-1/4" long point, eight middle at 21-3/8" long point, and eight inner at 11-7/8" short point, every end at 22.5deg off square — four of the boards each yield two outer and two middle pieces and the fifth yields all eight inner pieces, which is the only layout that fits five 8 ft boards. Cut the eight 26-1/4" deck trim pieces from the 1x4s, four per board, both ends beveled 22.5deg. For the hexagon variant, build six sections instead of eight and cut every 22.5deg angle at 30deg instead.

steps.title: Prestain all boards
steps.body: Prestain every board now, before a single joint goes together, including the ends and both faces. Once the eight sections are joined into an octagon the inside faces are genuinely hard to reach, and a bench that is half-finished on the inside will weather unevenly there first. Stand the parts on edge or on stickers to dry rather than flat, so the down face does not stick and lift.

steps.title: Build the leg sets
steps.body: Stand two 16-1/2" 4x4 legs on end and lay a 19" 2x4 top across them, one flush to each end of the cap, which leaves 12" of open space between the two legs. Drive three 2-3/4" exterior screws through the cap into the end grain of each leg, spreading them across the 3-1/2" width rather than in a line so the joint cannot pivot. Build eight of these — six for the hexagon variant — and stack them together as you go to check that all eight came out the same height, since a leg set that is even 1/8" tall will show as a step in the finished deck. With the 1-1/2" cap and the 1-1/2" deck boards on top, each set puts the finished seat at 19-1/2".

steps.title: Attach the leg set sides
steps.body: Attach an 18" beveled side piece to each side of every leg set, mirrored so the two 22.5deg bevels lean toward each other — this pair of bevels is what lets eight sections meet edge to edge and close a full octagon, so getting the mirroring right on every set matters more than the fastening. Run the long point of each bevel to the outside face. Fasten with 2-3/4" exterior screws into the legs. Dry-hold two finished leg sets against each other before building the rest and confirm the bevels meet flush with no gap at the outside; if they do not, correct the saw angle now rather than after all eight are built.

steps.title: Add the blocking
steps.body: Fit a 3-1/2" 2x4 block inside each leg set, between the top cap and the two side pieces, and screw through the cap and both sides into it with 2-3/4" exterior screws. This block is what turns a cap-and-two-sides joint into a solid corner — without it the sides can rack against the legs under someone sitting down and eventually work the screws loose. One block per set, eight in total.

steps.title: Dry-fit and assemble the deck
steps.body: Set all the leg sets out on flat, level ground in their octagon and lay the deck boards across them dry — the 26-1/4" outer ring, the 21-3/8" middle ring, and the 11-7/8" inner ring — before any deck fastener goes in. Check that each ring's mitered ends meet cleanly all the way round and that the accumulated error has not opened one joint into a wedge, and set the gap between the three rings now so it is consistent everywhere. This dry-fit is the whole reason for building on the ground first: laying up blind around the actual tree hides exactly the errors that are easiest to fix here and hardest to fix there. For the hexagon variant every deck end is cut at 30deg instead of 22.5deg.

steps.title: Assemble around the tree
steps.body: Carry the sections to the tree and set them around the trunk in the order you dry-fitted them, then fasten the deck boards down to the leg-set caps with 2-3/4" exterior screws, two per board per bearing point. Work around the ring rather than finishing one section at a time, so any accumulated error spreads out instead of piling up in the last joint. Check the top for level as you go and shim under a leg set where the ground falls away. Leave clearance between the inner ring and the bark all the way round — the trunk will grow into whatever gap you leave.

steps.title: Add the deck trim
steps.body: Fasten the eight 26-1/4" 1x4 trim pieces around the outer edge of the deck, their 22.5deg bevels meeting at each corner, with 2-3/4" exterior screws. The trim covers the outer deck boards' end grain, which is where an outdoor bench takes on water first, so seat each piece tight to the deck edge and touch up the stain on any face you cut or scuffed during assembly.

images.alt: Octagonal wooden bench built around a tree trunk
